paper_name: Development and Validation of a Deep Learning Model for Non	6Small Cell Lung Cancer Survival - PMC.pdf
title: Development and Validation of a Deep Learning Model for Non	6Small Cell Lung Cancer Survival
research_question: Can deep learning architecture be applied for individual prognosis evaluation and treatment recommendation in non	6small cell lung cancer (NSCLC)?
population: 17,322 patients with newly diagnosed stages I to IV NSCLC from the SEER database (2010-2015) and an external test cohort of 1,182 patients with stage I to III NSCLC from Shanghai Pulmonary Hospital (2009-2013).
study_design: Population-based cohort study with model development, internal validation, and external validation.
intervention_or_exposure: Application of a deep learning survival neural network model (DeepSurv) incorporating 127 clinical features including patient characteristics, tumor stage, and treatment strategies.
comparison: Tumor, node, and metastasis (TNM) staging system and Cox proportional hazard regression model.
main_finding: The DeepSurv model predicted lung cancer	6specific survival more accurately than the TNM staging system (C statistic 0.739 vs 0.706 in SEER test set; 0.742 vs 0.706 in external CHINA test set; both P < .001). Patients receiving treatments recommended by DeepSurv had significantly better survival (HR 2.99; 95% CI, 2.49-3.59; P < .001).
effect_type: Hazard ratio for survival benefit of recommended treatment
plain_summary: This study developed and validated a deep learning model (DeepSurv) to predict survival and recommend treatments for patients with non	6small cell lung cancer. Using large clinical datasets, the model outperformed traditional TNM staging in predicting lung cancer	6specific survival. Patients who received treatments recommended by the model had significantly better survival outcomes, suggesting that deep learning can provide personalized prognostic information and guide treatment decisions in clinical practice.
normalized_intervention_or_exposure: Deep learning survival neural network model (DeepSurv)
normalized_comparison: TNM staging system and Cox proportional hazards model

title: Smoking and Carcinoma of the Lung: Preliminary Report
research_question: Is there an association between tobacco smoking and carcinoma of the lung?
population: Patients admitted to 20 London hospitals with carcinoma of the lung, stomach, colon, or rectum, and a control group of non-cancer patients matched by sex and age.
study_design: Observational case-control study with hospital-based patient interviews.
intervention_or_exposure: Tobacco smoking habits, including amount smoked, type of tobacco (cigarettes or pipe), inhalation, and smoking history.
comparison: Non-cancer control patients matched by sex and age, with similar social class and place of residence considered.
outcome: Diagnosis of carcinoma of the lung confirmed by necropsy, biopsy, or clinical criteria.
main_finding: A strong association was found between smoking and carcinoma of the lung. The vast majority of lung carcinoma patients were smokers, with significantly fewer non-smokers compared to controls. Heavier smoking was more common among lung carcinoma patients. Cigarette smoking was more strongly associated with lung carcinoma than pipe smoking. Inhalation was slightly less common among lung carcinoma patients but not significantly different after adjustment.
effect_value: 0.3% of male lung carcinoma patients were non-smokers compared to 4.2% in controls; significant differences in smoking intensity and history between cases and controls.
p_value: P < 0.001 for differences in smoking prevalence and intensity between lung carcinoma patients and controls
plain_summary: This study investigated the relationship between smoking and lung cancer by interviewing patients with lung carcinoma and matched controls without cancer in London hospitals. The results showed a strong link between smoking and lung cancer, with most lung cancer patients being smokers, especially heavy smokers. Cigarette smoking was more strongly linked to lung cancer than pipe smoking. These findings support the hypothesis that smoking is a major cause of lung cancer.
normalized_comparison: Non-cancer control patients matched by sex and age
